coredata.dc:title: Chitosan gel loaded with carbon dots and mesoporous hydroxyapatite nanoparticles as a topical formulation for skin regeneration: An animal study

Ansari, Legha
coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon dots (CDs) are the research hotspots in antimicrobial applications. Also, due to the importance of calcium ions in skin regeneration, mesoporous hydroxyapatite nanoparticles (mHAP NPs) were prepared. This study aimed to evaluate the wound healing efficiency of both CD and mHAP NPs simultaneously.
Methods We doped Fluorine (F) and Copper (Cu) in CDs to prepare Cu*F-doped CDs (Cu.CDs). For the rapid release of calcium ions, a novel double-surfactant system (CTAB/F-127) was used for the synthesis of mHAP NPs. Then, we loaded both Cu.CDs and mHAP NPs simultaneously into the chitosan (CTS) gel.
Results The released calcium for mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 was proved about 30 % higher than that prepared using only CTAB, after one day. The BET-specific surface area for mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 was 23.17 % more than that prepared using only CTAB. This high porosity improves wound exudate absorption. Rheological analysis showed that the addition of both mHAP NPs and Cu.CD decreases the viscosity of CTS gel and thereby makes it suitable for topical applications. CTS gel containing mHAP + Cu.CDs indicated exceptional antibacterial properties even without light and/or H2O2 with an inhibition zone diameter of 35 and 17 mm for E. coli and S. aureus species, respectively. CTS gel containing mHAP + Cu.CDs also showed significantly higher wound closure efficacy, greater inflammation inhibition, and better re-epithelialization than the other groups in rats with full-thickness skin wounds.
Conclusion The CTS gel containing both Cu.CD and mHAP NPs can be proposed as a new therapeutic gel for wound healing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the research hotspots in antimicrobial applications. Also, due to the importance of calcium ions in skin regeneration, mesoporous hydroxyapatite nanoparticles (mHAP NPs) were prepared. This study aimed to evaluate the wound healing efficiency of both CD and mHAP NPs simultaneously.

We doped Fluorine (F) and Copper (Cu) in CDs to prepare Cu*F-doped CDs (Cu.CDs). For the rapid release of calcium ions, a novel double-surfactant system (CTAB/F-127) was used for the synthesis of mHAP NPs. Then, we loaded both Cu.CDs and mHAP NPs simultaneously into the chitosan (CTS) gel.

The released calcium for mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 was proved about 30 % higher than that prepared using only CTAB, after one day. The BET-specific surface area for mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 was 23.17 % more than that prepared using only CTAB. This high porosity improves wound exudate absorption. Rheological analysis showed that the addition of both mHAP NPs and Cu.CD decreases the viscosity of CTS gel and thereby makes it suitable for topical applications. CTS gel containing mHAP + Cu.CDs indicated exceptional antibacterial properties even without light and/or H2O2 with an inhibition zone diameter of 35 and 17 mm for E. coli and S. aureus species, respectively. CTS gel containing mHAP + Cu.CDs also showed significantly higher wound closure efficacy, greater inflammation inhibition, and better re-epithelialization than the other groups in rats with full-thickness skin wounds.
The CTS gel containing both Cu.CD and mHAP NPs can be proposed as a new therapeutic gel for wound healing.

Data contained in the text of the article and referenced in the article may allow other researchers to reuse this data in the following links:

ce:section.ce:para: The skin, with its physical and immunological properties, plays an essential role as an important immune barrier against environmental pathogens and thermal changes (Sanford and Gallo, 2013; Gantwerker and Hom, 2012). Any damage to the normal anatomical structure of the skin or loss of its normal function is defined as a wound (Mc, 2001). If a wound does not heal, becomes infected, or develops additional scarring or deformity (Hunt and Hopf, 1997). Therefore, much effort has been focused on wound care with an emphasis on new antimicrobial systems or the development of new technologies for acute and chronic wound management (Velnar et al., 2009).

Chitosan (CTS), the most prevalent biopolymer in the natural world after cellulose, is widely employed in wound management as its relevant products have previously been marketed (TegadermTM Film) (Muzzarelli, 1993; Muzzarelli, 1999; Muzzarelli, 2007). CTS in organic organisms can be gradually depolymerized to N-acetylglucosamine, which starts the proliferation of fibroblasts and facilitates the deposition of ordered collagen (Ansari, 2023). It needs to be mentioned that CTS degradation products do not accumulate in the body, are non-toxic, and do not activate immune responses (Karri, 2016). The positive charge of the polymer is liable for its antibacterial properties, which stimulate its interaction with anionic groups on the surface of gram-negative and gram-positive cells and the formation of an impermeable layer around bacteria, which ultimately leads to preventing the transfer of vital nutrients to the cells and cell death occurs (Derakhshi, 2023). One limitation of CTS when it is applied as a based polymer for the preparation of topical gel is the high viscosity and non-spreadability of its highly concentrated aqueous solution. Some new molecular interactions as well as some cleavages in the chemical bonds in mixed solutions of polymers, surfactants, and excipients can affect the rheological and physicochemical properties of solutions. Here, we used some nanosized additives to address the limitation of the CTS solution and also to impart some other advantages to the gel.

Carbon dots (CDs) are spherical carbon-based nanoparticles with a size of around 10 nm, which have gained tremendous attention in medical science due to their exceptional physicochemical peripeties (Miao, 2020; Chung et al., 2020). Nowadays, CDs are widely utilized as antibacterial agents in skin regeneration owing to their biocompatibility and degradability (Li, 2020; Cui, 2021; Wang, 2020). Compared to the traditional antibiotic, CDs indicate less bacterial resistance due to enhanced membrane permeability, lack of efflux pump, and induction of various antibacterial mechanisms. Recently, various heteroatoms, including N, S, P, F, Cl, and metal elements such as Cu, Co, Ni, and so on, have been applied to improve their properties and their applications (Ansari, 2021). Heteroatoms can increase the catalytic properties of CDs by affecting the structural defects and charge transitions of CDs. For example, Cu-doped CDs catalyzed the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) via a Fenton-like reaction, leading to enhancing the antibacterial properties of CDs. Bacteria are attacked by produced ROS via different mechanisms, such as the degradation of DNA and cell membranes (Nichols, 2020; Wang, 2022). F and N single-doped CDs were also utilized as antibacterial agents, and their antibacterial mechanisms were studied. (Liang, 2021). In addition, copper is a vital metal and is needed in low amounts in many metabolic processes (Borkow, 2010; Uauy et al., 1998). In wound healing, by controlling its quantities, copper plays a critical role by stimulating the expression of some extracellular matrix molecules, such as collagen, fibrinogen, and integrins, as well as the main mediators of cell attachment to the extracellular matrix (Borkow, 2010; Uauy et al., 1998; Tenaud, 1999; Sen, 2002). Based on that, in the present work, we doped copper ions in CDs to potent their antibacterial properties.

It has already been established that a geometrically well-organized scaffold can act as a template that effectively guides cells to the right location for highly ordered tissue deposition to occur. In other words, collagen deposition is affected by geometry as well as biomaterial composition, which are intimately combined and cross-correlated (Scaglione, 2012). Hydroxyapatite (HAP), Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2, is one of the fascinating bioceramics owing to its well-organized geometry, thermal stability, chemical activity, and high biocompatibility. As it is a ceramic, it has low tensile strength and poor mechanical properties (Ahmed, 2020; Elsayed, 2020). Due to high surface area and surface roughness, HAP nanoparticles (HAP NPs) are more stable and present enhanced resorbability and higher bioactivity, inducing greater cell adhesion and cell-matrix interactions (Molino, 2020). It is mostly useful in tissue regeneration, drug delivery, and wound healing (Derakhshi, 2023; Ansari, 2020). Mesoporous structures have the ability to be easily functionalized with many molecules and ligands, not only by chemical bonding but also by physical embedding of components (Bagheri, 2021). Mesoporous HAP NPs (mHAP NPs) have an elongation structure with a large specific surface area, and their capability in tissue engineering and regeneration processes is increased. Scaglione et al. reported the bone tissue deposition within the pores of a HAP scaffold with regular and ordered inner geometry and collagen fibers were deposited within concentric layers in a parallel arrangement (Scaglione, 2012). Tao et al. also reported that HAP NPs motivate collagen deposition for infected wound repairing and skin reconstruction (Tao, 2021).

Because of the aforementioned advantages of CTS in wound healing utilizations over the other polymers, the CTS gel was prepared in this research to fabricate a new therapeutic gel for skin regeneration applications. According to some information, calcium ions manage intracellular signals that regulate several cellular activities. Also, this ion is employed in many signaling pathways vital for wound healing. For example, the influx of calcium into cells modulates inflammatory cell infiltration, keratinocyte migration, and fibroblast proliferation (Kawai, 2011; Subramaniam, 2021). In addition to calcium ions, other ions also help tissue regeneration. In 2022, Xu et al. informed us that the release of phosphorus and ferric ions significantly improved bone regeneration (Xu, 2022). According to these reports and the fact that HAP in a solution degrades over time and releases phosphorous and calcium ions, we hypothesized that the faster release of calcium and phosphorous ions from mHAP NPs could accelerate tissue regeneration (Wang, 2004). A nonionic surfactant Pluronic® F-127, also named Poloxamer 407 has many biomedical uses. Due to the advantages such as biocompatibility, easy availability, and, most importantly, being FDA-approved, we rationally used F-127 along with cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) in the synthesis process of mHAP NPs to increase the porosity and surface area, and consequently increase the release rate of calcium ions from the mHAP NPs. Therefore, for the first time, both inorganic Cu.CDs and organic mHAP NPs were loaded into CTS gel. Next, The changes in rheological properties of the as-prepared gel in the presence of NPs were investigated. Also, the antibacterial activity of the developed gel and its mechanism were investigated. Finally, the gel loaded with both Cu.CDs and mHAP NPs was applied to treat full-thickness skin wounds in rats.

Calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O; 95 %; Scharlau) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O; 98 %; BDH) were used for the preparation of m-HAP NPs. Purified non-ionic surfactant Pluronic® F-127 (EO100PO65EO100; EO = ethylene oxide, PO = propylene oxide; Sigma-Aldrich) and ionic surfactant cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB; Sigma-Aldrich) were purchased as micelle templates. The pH of the solutions was adjusted by sodium hydroxide (NaOH; 97 %; Fluca). Chitosan (medium molecular weight; Sigma-Aldrich), acetic acid glacial (Scharlau), and glycerol (Carl Roth) were used to prepare the gels. Ofloxacin, Cu(CH3COO)2, and Dialysis membrane (MWCO 2000) (purchased from Sigma) were applied for the synthesis of CDs. 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, Sigma) was used for investigating the peroxidase activity of CDs. Mueller-Hinton agar (Merck, Germany) was used for the in vitro antibacterial study. All chemicals were used as received without any modification. Deionized water was used as a solvent in all experiments. The bacterial stock cultures were received from Microlab, Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Urmia, Iran. The rats were purchased from the Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and Cu-doped CDs (Cu.CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 10.5 mg of ofloxacin, dissolved in 24 mL water, was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to ambient temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 8000 rpm, and then the supernatant solution containing N, F CDs was filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 µm). For more purification, the prepared CDs were dialyzed by a membrane (MWCO 2000) against deionized water. The concentration of the C-dots solution was calculated according to the concentration of carbon atoms in the carbon source (Liang, 2021). For the synthesis of N, F, and Cu CDs, 35 mg Cu(CH3COO)2 was added to the dissolved ofloxacin, and then the same procedure was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous hydroxyapatite nanoparticles (mHAP NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the hydrothermal method was used for the synthesis of mHAP NPs. In a typical procedure for the preparation of mHAP NPs, a solution of phosphate was prepared by dissolving 4 g CTAB and 0.4 g NaH2PO4·12H2O in 25 mL of deionized water. The solution of CaCl2·2H2O was separately prepared by dissolving 1.2 g of this substance in 25 mL of deionized water. The CaCl2 solution was added drop by drop to the phosphate solution under vigorous stirring at ambient temperature. Then the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 10.5 by the addition of the right amount of NaOH solution. Finally, this solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling it down at room temperature, the obtained milky solid product was centrifuged for 5 min at 9000 rpm and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol (Govindan, 2017). Last, the white pellet was resuspended in ethanol and sonicated for 40 min at 60 °C and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min for removal of all contained CTAB from produced mHAP NPs (Derakhshi, 2023). In the second method, we used the double surfactant method for the preparation of mHAP NPs. For that, pluronic F-127 was used to evaluate the effect of simultaneously employing two different kinds of ionic and non-ionic surfactants on the size, morphology, and calcium release rate of mHAP NPs. To this end, the aforementioned process was repeated by employing both CTAB and F-127 for the induction of mesoporous structure. Therefore, instead of 4 g CTAB, we dissolved 2 g Pluronic F-127 and 2 g CTAB for the preparation of the phosphate solution.

To evaluate the calcium release rate from mHAP NPs, the solutions with the same pH as the solution that will be used for the preparation of CTS gels were prepared. Equal values of mHAP NPs prepared by CTAB and CTAB/F-127 were added to the separate solutions. The entire volume of the release medium was collected by centrifuging and replaced with the same volume of the same solution (without mHAP NPs). The concentration of calcium ions in the collected media was determined using atomic absorption spectroscopy (Analytik Jena, novAA 400, Germany). Since the duration of our animal study will be 15 days, and each gel formulation must be used during this period, therefore the calcium release rate was evaluated for 15 days. All experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to more Ca2+ ion release, only the mHAP NPs synthesized in the presence of both CTAB and F-127 were used for further characterization. The analysis of size and morphology of mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs were conducted by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM; Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of mHAP NPs were determined via a surface area and porosity analyzer (BEL, Japan). The surface area was calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Also, the surface areas, pore volume, and pore size distribution were obtained from the desorption branches of the isotherms through the Barrett–Joyner–Halanda (BJH) method. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs were recorded by the FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Spectrum Two, Waltham, MA, USA) in the wavenumbers range of 400 to 4000 cm−1. XRD was performed using a GNR Analytical Instrument (Model: EXPLORER, CuKα = 1.541874 Å, Italy). Diffraction patterns were recorded over a 2θ range starting at approximately 4◦ and finishing at 80◦. The incremental step size used over the 2θ range was 0.010°. The surface charge of CDs and Cu.CDs was determined using a Zeta potential analyzer (Nanotrac Wave, Microtrac). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, Tescan Mira 3 FEG SEM, Czech) was used to determine the chemical elements present in CDs and Cu.CDs samples. The fluorescence and UV/vis spectra of CDs and Cu.CDs were recorded by F-2500 spectrofluorimeter (Hitachi, Japan) and SPEKOL 2000 spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena, Ger- many), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CTS gels containing mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Five kinds of CTS gels were prepared here for further evaluation of in-vitro antibacterial activity. For the preparation of blank CTS gel, 4 g of CTS powder was added little by little to 50 mL of 7 % acetic acid aqueous solution and stirred at normal temperature. Subsequently, 20 mL glycerol and 40 mL deionized water were added into the CTS solution with stirring for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous CTS gel. Five kinds of CTS gels were prepared in this work, including CTS gel containing mHAP NPs, CTS gel containing CDs, CTS gel containing Cu.CDs, CTS gel containing mHAP NPs and CDs, and CTS gel containing mHAP NPs and Cu.CDs. These different gels are denoted in this work as CTS/HAP, CTS/CD, CTS/Cu.CD, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD, respectively. For the preparation of gel containing mHAP NPs, following the aforementioned process for blank CTS gel, mHAP NPs were added into the CTS solution in an amount of 20 %wt of the total polymer mass, and the dispersion was stirred for another 2 h. By adding 60 mL CD and Cu.CD (equal to 0.03 g CD and Cu.CDs, respectively) into the CTS/HAP gel under continuous stirring for 4 h, the CTS/HAP + CD and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gels are prepared. All the CTS gels were stored in tightly closed containers at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological properties of CTS gels containing mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The rheological properties of the test gels were investigated using a compact rheometer (MCR300 from Anton Paar, Austria). The rheogram of each gel was obtained at a constant temperature of 25 °C.

The bacterial species employed for the antibacterial test were Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli). The bacterial isolates were fully grown overnight at 37 °C in Mueller Hinton Agar at pH 7.4. The well diffusion method was applied for screening of in vitro antibacterial activity of the CTS gels. 0.5 McFarland turbidity standard bacterial suspension of S. aureus and E. coli were seeded in plates, followed by the distribution of nutrient agar medium. Four wells of 5 mm in diameter were cut into the agar media of each plate, and then gels in 60 µl volumes containing 10.5 µg of each CD were adjoined into the wells. Inoculated plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and zones of inhibition were measured in mm. Three replicates were conducted for each bacterium.

The skin regeneration efficacy of different types of CTS gels prepared in this work was evaluated using forty-five Wistar male rats (n = 9 in each group) weighing 300–350 g. All animals were housed in the Animal House of Urmia University of Medical Sciences in a controlled condition (12 h light/dark cycle and temperature of 21 °C) with free access to water and food. The animals were allowed to become accustomed to the laboratory environment for about one week before commencing the in vivo experiment. At the end of the in vivo study, the rats were sacrificed with an overdose of ketamine/xylazine mixture. It is need to be mentioned that all animal experiments were accomplished on account of the protocol approved by the National Institutes of Animal Care and use guidelines approved by the Institutional Ethics Committee (IEC) at Urmia University of Medical Sciences (IR.UMSU.REC.1399.390) (Ahmadi-Noorbakhsh, 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound model and skin regeneration
ce:section.ce:para:
On the day of the injury, the dorsal sides of all rats were shaved and sterilized with 70 % ethanol. Subsequently, a mixture of ketamine (100 mg/kg)/ xylazine (10 mg/kg) was administrated intraperitoneally for anesthesia. Since this is a painful procedure, we also used tramadol as an analgesic before the skin incision. Following that, circular regions of the animals' posterior with a 15 mm in diameter were removed. The day of the injury was accounted as the zero-day. The rats were randomly divided into five groups, with nine rats in each group. CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gels plus the blank and the control (Ctrl) were the five tested groups. The CTS gel and the sterile cotton gauze were used to cover the wound site of the blank and Ctrl rats, respectively. Three time-points (days 5, 10, and 15) were selected for assessment of the skin regeneration efficacy of gels. At each time-point, three rats from each experimental group were randomly selected, and after photographing the wound of each rat, the wound area was measured using a vernier caliper. Also, to track the skin regeneration process from a pathological point of view, the wounded skin biopsies were collected at each time-point. The mean wound area (%) was calculated by the following equation (Derakhshi, 2023):

In the aforementioned equation, day A is one of the three time-points, and day 0 is the day of the injury.

Three animals from each experimental group were sacrificed on the 5th, 10th, and 15th day post-injury, and the wound bed, besides the surrounding healed skin, was collected for histological analysis. The biopsies were first fixed in 10 % buffered formalin, then dehydrated with ethanol and inserted in paraffin sections. Following that, H&E was used to stain the biopsies with a thickness of 6 μm and observed by optical microscope at 10x magnifications (OLYMPUS, model CX31, Japan) (Ansari, 2021).

Version 9.1.2.226 of GraphPad Prism was applied for the statistical analysis of data. Different data received by physicochemical characterization, antibacterial, and in vivo studies were analyzed by t-test or two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Tukey’s post-test. The significance level of 0.05 was used for statistical analysis. Results were represented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). To display the size distribution of CDs and Cu.CDs as histograms the software IBM SPSS Statistics version 27 was used.

CDs and Cu.CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. Ofloxacin was applied as a C, N, and F precursor, and copper acetate was applied as a Cu precursor. TEM imaging (Supplementary Figure S1) indicates the formation of monodispersed and spherical CDs and Cu.CDs with sizes around 19 and 32 nm, respectively. The FT-IR and EDS analyses were exploited to justify the surface functional groups and chemical compounds of prepared CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Supplementary Figure S2) indicates the existence of C=O, C=N, C−N, and N−H moieties at the surface of CDs whose related bands appeared at 1636, 1524, 1464, and 1384 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic peaks of C-F appeared at 1269 and 1052 cm−1. The broad bands at 3462 and 3242 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of −OH and −NH (Liang, 2021). Appearing the new peak around 1100 cm−1 (attributed to the N-Cu–N stretching vibration) and decreasing the peaks at 1524, 1464, and 1384 cm−1 (related to C=N, C-N, and N–H groups) in Cu.CDs, compared to CDs, confirm the formation of Cu coordination complexes in Cu.CDs (Wang, 2022; Nemati, 2023). Furthermore, the EDS results (Fig. 1 ) confirm the exitance of C, O, N, F, and Cu elements in the structure of Cu.CDs. Zeta analysis showed the surface charge of CDs and Cu.CDs is + 0.6 ± 0.02 and + 178.1 ± 3.14 mv, indicating that the positive charge of CDs increased after doping with Cu2+ (Wang, 2022). The optical properties of both CDs were investigated by recording the fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra of the CDs (Supplementary Figure S3). Figures S3a and S3b indicate the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and Cu.CDs excited with various wavelengths (300–380 nm). Both prepared CDs indicated excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence behavior with the maximum emission intensity at 500 nm. With doping Cu in the structure of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched, which is in good agreement with already reported studies (Nichols, 2020). As can be seen at the UV–Vis spectra of both CDs (Supplementary Figure S3c), the π −π* n-π* transition assigned characteristic peaks of CDs appeared around 220 (attributed to C=C), 290 (attributed to C=N and C=O) and 330 nm (attributed to C−F).

We used two different procedures for the synthesis of mHAP NPs. At first method, we used only CTAB as the template in the synthesis of a porous structure. In the second method, we used pluronic F-127 in addition to CTAB. The FTIR spectrum of the mHAP NP and its description are embedded in Supplementary Figure S4. Supplementary Figure S4 The XRD pattern of the mHAP NP and its description are inserted in Supplementary Figure S5.

mHAP NPs prepared using the CTAB and CTAB/F-127 were subjected to the calcium release test. As expressed in Table 1 , the concentrations of calcium ions in the release media of mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 are significantly higher than those prepared using CTAB (P < 0.05). The amount of drug released after one day of incubation with the release medium was about 30 % more in the case of mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 than mHAP NPs prepared using the CTAB. Therefore, mixed CTAB/F-127 was chosen for the preparation of mHAP NPs, which were characterized in the next steps.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and the associated BJH pore size distribution of the mHAP NPs are shown in Fig. 2 . The curve of the mHAP NPs is revealed as a type IV isotherm with a type H1 hysteresis loop, which proves the mesoporous structure (Thommes, 2015). The BET-specific surface area was 84.314 and 64.775 m2 g−1, the BJH method cumulative desorption pore volume was 0.38 and 0.43 cm3 g−1, and the BJH method desorption pore diameter was 1.66 and 7 nm for mHAP NPs prepared using the CTAB/F-127 and CTAB, respectively. The BET mean pore diameter was 17.738 and 25.561 nm for mHAP NPs prepared using the CTAB/F-127 and CTAB, respectively.

The particle size of mHAP NPs prepared using CTAB/F-127 is presented in Supplementary Figure S6. A double surfactant system resulted in NPs with shorter lengths rather than the employment of a single surfactant. It seems that F-127 micelles hinder the NPs from growing into large particles. As depicted in this figure, the diameter and the length of most mHAP NPs measured using Image-J 1.52n and IBM SPSS Statistics software were around 19.23 ± 1.87 and 59.35 ± 5.80 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CTS gels containing mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to mHAP NPs, water absorption also depends on the hydrophilicity of the polymer. Since CTS is a hydrophilic polymer, we decided to use it to form therapeutic gels to take advantage of its water absorption capacity. We loaded the synthesized NPs into the CTS gels for further antibacterial and in vivo assessments. We prepared four types of gels as follows: CTS, CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CDs, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CDs for antibacterial studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CTS gels containing mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 , the viscosity study of the gels exhibited a pseudoplastic behavior so that by increasing the shear stress the viscosity decreased. Except for formulation CTS/HAP + Cu.CDs, the viscosity of other formulations is increased by the addition of NPs. In a constant shear stress, the viscosity change is as follows: CTS/HAP + CDs > CTS/HAP > CTS > CTS/HAP + Cu.CDs.

In this study, for a better understanding of the antibacterial effect and possible synergistic effect of mHAP NPs and CDs doped with different antibacterial agents, fluorine, nitrogen, and copper, we explored the antibacterial properties of various kinds of gels containing these NPs, including CTS/HAP, CTS/CD, CTS/Cu.CD, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD. The antibacterial activity of developed gels was investigated on E. coli (gram-negative) and S. aureus (gram-positive) as the typical bacteria models. The results are tabulated in Table 2 as mean values of inhibition zone diameter. As well, the illustration of the gels’ antibacterial activity is presented in Fig. 4 . The results show that all the gels containing CDs have shown extraordinary antibacterial activity against E. coli. However, the antibacterial activity is more restricted against S. aureus. So that the CTS/HAP, CTS/CD, and CTS/HAP + CD gels showed very little effect, then we considered their inhibition zone diameter to be zero.

As can be seen in Fig. 5 , the best antibacterial activity was observed for Cu.CD containing gels, especially on the gram-negative bacteria, E. coli. To confirm these results, we used the excellent fluorescence properties of CDs and the fluorescence spectra of Cu.CDs and CDs were recorded in the presence and absence of both examined bacteria. For this purpose, the CDs were incubated with bacteria on the shaker incubator for 1 h, and then the solution was centrifuged for the separation of bacteria, and the fluorescence spectrum of the supernatant was measured (Liang, 2021). As can be seen in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of Cu.CDs decreased in the presence of bacteria, confirming the entrance of Cu.CDs to the bacteria. The most decrease in fluorescence intensity was obtained for Cu.CDs in the presence of E. coli, indicating the more efficient interaction between Cu.CDs and E. coli. The negligible effect of both bacteria on the fluorescence intensity of CDs indicated less interaction between CDs and bacteria compared to Cu.CDs (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the peroxidase-like activity of CDs was studied by TMB chromogenic assay. As shown in Fig. 5c, the appearance of a deeper blue color in the solution containing Cu.CDs indicated that the peroxidase-like activity of Cu.CDs is much more intensive than CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study and pathology analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The circles with diameters of 15 mm were harvested from the skin of the back of the rats to establish full-thickness wounds. The CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gels plus the Ctrl and blank were the five tested groups on which the wound healing potential of prepared gels was examined. All gels were suitable for skin care and showed no irritancy effect. The skin regeneration efficiency of different gels was explored at three time-points following injury (days 5, 10, and 15). Each examined group contained 9 rats (3 rats for each time point). The wound site of the Ctrl rats was wrapped with cotton gauze. The wound of blank rats was covered with CTS gel. However, as depicted in Fig. 6
, pictures prepared by photographing the wound site revealed that wounds treated by CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gels are in better condition compared to Ctrl and blank groups. Among different groups, wounds treated by CTS/HAP + Cu.CD are in better health and were healed significantly faster than the other groups (P < 0.05, Fig. 7 ), and on the 15th day post-operation, these treated wounds had almost healed entirely (Fig. 6). The statistical difference between different groups at three time-points is tabulated in Table 3 .

The mean wound area in CTS/HAP + Cu.CD, CTS/HAP + CD, CTS/HAP, blank, and Ctrl groups were 5.01 ± 0.07 %, 6.35 ± 0.06 %, 6.47 ± 0.04 %, 23.65 ± 0.26, and 42.13 ± 0.09 %, respectively, at the last time point (day 15) that showed a significant difference in the mean wound area between different groups (except CTS/HAP vs. CTS/HAP + CD, P < 0.05, Fig. 7). According to Table 3, this difference in the mean wound area is also significant between different groups at the other two time points (except CTS/HAP + CD vs. CTS/HAP + Cu.CD at the first time point, P < 0.05).

These aforementioned findings are in conformity with the histological analysis of the wounds at three examined time points (Fig. 8 ). On day 5, the pathological images displayed an immense presence of engorged blood vessels (EBV) as well as inflammatory cells (IC), including polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs or neutrophils) and monocytes in the wound site of all groups except CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group. As is clear, inflammatory cells are much lower in this group. Granulation tissue (GT) is present in the base of the wound and helps in the filling and contraction of the wounds and epithelial cell migration. As the healing process continued, GT was gradually turned into fibrous connective tissue. At this time point, the healthy pink GT is observed in CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group (Fig. 8). The GT of the Ctrl group is observed even until the last day of study. From the standpoint of re-epithelialization, the epithelial tissue didn't form in Ctrl and blank groups, whereas a very thin and non-continuous layer of neo-epithelium is beginning to create around the wounded tissue of CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD groups. On day 10, once again, the wound site of the Ctrl and blank groups is full of monocytes. The volume of the inflammation decreased in three other groups. However, again the re-epithelialization didn't cover the wound surface of the Ctrl and blank groups. On the contrary, the CTS/HAP + CD- and CTS/HAP-treated wounds were in the process of producing thin layers of neo-epithelium and keratin, and the epidermis got more organized at this time point. Most epidermis organization and the thickest layer of the epithelium are observed in the wound site of CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group. More importantly, the wounds were bandaged using CTS/HAP + Cu.CD on the 10th day reconstructs some skin structures, such as hair follicles and sebaceous glands, which are the main signs of fibroblast and keratinocyte cells’ proliferation for expanding the connective tissue and collagen and remarkable regeneration of epidermis and dermis layers. On day 15, there were still monocytes and EBVs in the wound site of the Ctrl and blank groups, although their numbers were reduced compared to the first days of skin restoration. No inflammation is observed in the wound site of the three other main groups. On this day, the re-epithelialization is almost formed in all studied groups. The progress in the regeneration process is demonstrated by resurfacing the wound site with the quite clear epidermis and keratinocyte layers, along with the formation of sebaceous glands in the CTS/HAP + CD and CTS/HAP groups. The most organized epidermis and dermis, as well as complete tissue remodeling, is observed in the CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group, which shows the morphology and structure near the normal skin.

Calcium phosphate nanoparticles play an important role in keratinocyte proliferation and differentiation as well as in skin homeostasis (Kawai, 2011). The chemical structure of HAP is similar to bone bioapatite and has wide applications in biomedicine. HAP promotes gene expression and collagen protein synthesis. According to some information, cell behaviors such as cell spreading and proliferation, as well as vital nutrients and oxygen transfer, and waste product transportation are affected by porosity (Mohammadpour, 2021). Therefore, two main purposes in the synthesis of mHAP NPs were to fabricate a rod-like structure to provide a suitable template for the deposition of the cells together with the components of the extracellular matrix, then to fabricate a porous structure for manipulation of cell behavior and enhance the wound exudate absorption. In addition, the controllable release of calcium ions is affected by the porosity. Previous studies acknowledged that the calcium ions considerably affect fibroblast contraction and proliferation that aid wound closure (Kawai, 2011; Wang, 2015). Accordingly, we set ‘calcium ion release’ as the main criterion for deciding on the choice of synthesis method. CTAB is commonly used for the preparation of mesoporous structures. Pluronics are polymeric surfactants, and their micellar behavior is typically quite different from that of conventional surfactants due to the considerable amount of hydration of their monomeric units in the aqueous phase. Their micelles are very compact and much larger than conventional micelles (Bakshi, 2016). For this reason, NPs that are nucleated in the presence of pluronic are not allowed to grow into larger ones. Compared to the single surfactant system for mHAP preparation, double-surfactant systems exhibit a higher potential for shape control because of better interfacial adsorption capability. The double-surfactant system, ionic and nonionic, makes the structure more porous, so the structure will be less mechanically stable. As it is determined in the BET analysis, the high surface area of the mHAP NPs prepared using the CTAB/F-127, due to the high porosity, causes more surface to be in contact with the release medium and therefore the apatite structure breaks down faster. On the other hand, because of the high porosity, the apatite structure becomes more unstable and more prone to destruction. Unlike drug delivery systems where the sustained release of drugs is preferred, wound healing systems require rapid drug release for effective and faster tissue regeneration (Nasirizadeh, 2020). Accordingly, the mixed CTAB/F-127 was chosen for the preparation of mHAP NPs, in this study.

The surface area and porosity of a structure are fundamentally vital parameters for cell attraction and tissue deposition in wound healing. Scaglione et al. reported that the porous structure can guide the bone tissue to depose within the pores. Furthermore, our previous study proved that the water absorption ability of the wound dressing depends on the porosity (Derakhshi, 2023). The addition of mHAP NPs to the wound dressing increased the water uptake capacity by more than 2.5 times (Derakhshi, 2023). Increasing the efficiency of a therapeutic system for draining wound exudation and, in parallel, preventing scar formation is guaranteed by creating a wet environment during skin wound closure. Therefore, high surface area and porous morphology are key characteristics for both exudate uptake and tissue remodeling. Considering the above, we analyzed the mHAP NPs synthesized in this work from the point of view of porosity and surface area. The larger specific surface area, which is attributed to the more porosity of the mHAP NPs prepared by CTAB/F-127, is very beneficial for the absorption of a significant amount of the wound exudate and the commencement of cell attachment and proliferation. Therefore, the smart choice of a double-surfactant system, CTAB/F-127, increased the surface area of these NPs and also increased the porosity of the structure..

Due to the bioadhesion capability of CTS, this polymer is one of the most interesting biopolymers used in some biomedical applications such as sutureless surgery, wound repairing, and therapeutic topical formulations (Hamedi et al., 2022). Accordingly, in the current paper, CTS-based gels containing NPs were prepared as a wound-healing agent. Polymer solutions including various excipients can exhibit some interactions or some interaction cleavages as well as intramolecular and polymer–solvent interactions, that affect the physicochemical and rheological behaviors of the solutions. Here, the rheological behaviors of formulations were significantly affected by the addition of mHAP NPs, CDs, and Cu.CDs. The presence of NPs may create some new interactions between NPs and polymer or solvent, as well as cross-linking of polymer by mHAP NPs or CDs. The reduction in viscosity for CTS/HAP + Cu.CDs is attributed to the distribution of metallic NP within the polymer network, which fractures some polymer–polymer interactions, and in turn, reduces flow resistance. In general, the results showed that the CTS-based gels are good platforms for topical applications.

Fluoride is a great inorganic element that disrupts bacterial metabolism by blocking glycolytic enzymes (Wiegand et al., 2007; Marquis, 1995). The effective antibacterial activity of F- ions using several fluoride composites, such as calcium fluoride (CaF2), has been previously studied and showed both biocompatibility and antibacterial properties (Kulshrestha, 2016; Jeong, 2018). Contrary to antibiotics, metal ions (Mn+) act in another way on bacteria due to the digression of metal transport systems from normal patterns. This allows M−containing nanomaterials to be used as long-term antibacterial materials without remarkable harm to the body (Godoy-Gallardo, 2021). Among metal NPs, copper (Cu) plays a key role in many kinds of cells. In tissue regeneration, the mechanism of action of many factors, such as various cytokines and growth factors, is regulated by the interaction with Cu ions. Cu is effectively involved in all phases of the wound healing process (Kornblatt et al., 2016; Salvo and Sandoval, 2022). For example, it reduces the probability of infections and accelerates the passage of the inflammation phase. Moreover, it can promote the formation of new blood vessels in the wound area (Tao, 2021; Sukhodub, 2023). Accordingly, in the present work, we doped copper ions in CDs to potent their antibacterial properties.

In other studies, in the field of wounds, Chinnasamy and coworkers produced an antibacterial hydrogel using PF127 polymer, Azadirachta indica (AI) extract, and silver nanoparticles (AI-AgNPs-PF127). They reported that AI-AgNPs (before loading in the polymer) produced a zone of inhibition against Escherichia coli (18.7 mm), and Staphylococcus aureus (17.7 mm) (Chinnasamy et al., 2021 Feb). In the other study by Ferreira et al. disc diffusion test against Staphylococcus aureus, the inhibition halo was observed for both CTS with 2 % Chlorhexidine and CTS with 4 % Chlorhexidine was 24 mm (Ferreira et al., 2019). As the antibacterial effect of positive control ciprofloxacin exists in the literature, we ignore the repeat test in this study. The inhibition zone of ciprofloxacin alone was 16.00 ± 2.12 mm for Staphylococcus aureus (Ibraheem et al., 2022). The other study revealed that nanosilver-loaded silk fibroin gel (6 % w/v) exhibited an area of inhibition of 11 mm (Patil et al., 2015).

The better antibacterial activity of Cu.CD containing gels, especially on the gram-negative bacteria, E. coli. can be attributed to the strong electrostatic interaction between Cu.CD with high positive surface charge (ζCu.CDs = +178.1 ± 3.14 mV ≫ ζCDs = +0.6 ± 0.02) and bacteria membranes with a negative surface charge. Compared to E. coli., the surface charge of S. aureus is more positive, and its cytoderm is thicker, so the interaction and entrance of CDs into S. aureus is more difficult than E. coli (Wang, 2022). It has already been reported that nanomaterials with a positive potential and small particle size are able to pass through the bacteria membranes (Wang, 2022; Sun, 2021; Li and Kang, 2018). We suspected that due to small size, good dispersity, and positive surface potential, Cu.CDs could be inserted into the bacterial cells and disturb intercellular reaction procedures. The result of the fluorescence intensity analysis also confirmed our proposed mechanism. The other mechanism for the antibacterial activity of Cu.CDs can be attributed to their mimic peroxidase activity, which can increase the production of free oxygen radicals. It has been reported that endogenous H2O2 was oxidized and transformed to free radicals such as •OH through a Cu.CD-based enzymatic reaction (Wang, 2022). The evaluation of the peroxidase-like activity indicated that the peroxidase-like activity of Cu.CDs is much more intensive than CDs. Cu.CDs can catalyze the generating of free radicals such as •OH, which are able to cause considerable oxidative damage to the cytomembrane and important small molecules of bacteria. Compared to the already reported CDs, in which the existence of H2O2 or UV light was necessary for activating their antibacterial property (Nichols, 2020; Tripathi, 2020; Liang, 2020), Cu.CDs indicated exceptional antibacterial activity without additional light and/or H2O2. Thus, Cu.CDs can be applied as efficient and promising antibacterial agents.

Wound healing includes four specific, but overlapping phases of hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling. In brief, at the hemostasis phase, platelets are activated, adhere, and accumulate at the site of the wound. Platelets release the growth factors, cyclic AMP, and adhesive glycoproteins. As platelet aggregation continues, coagulation factors are released, which leads to the deposition of a fibrin clot at the site of injury. The inflammation phase is managed by neutrophils and macrophages. The bacteria and denatured matrix components that delay healing are removed by neutrophils and macrophages. These inflammatory cells are the second source of growth factors and cytokines. Prolonged inflammation phase delays healing due to inordinate amounts of proteases and reactive oxygen that damage vital factors. In the proliferation phase, fibroblasts proliferate and produce ECM irregularly. Also, the basal epithelial cells proliferate and migrate over the granulation tissue for closure of the injury surface. In the remodeling phase, fibroblast and capillary density are reduced, and the original scar tissue is removed and replaced with ECM that is more similar to normal skin. ECM remodeling is the result of the balanced and regulated activity of proteases (Ahmadi et al., 2015; Schultz et al., 2011).

Based on the explanation given above, what motivates cell proliferation and tissue deposition is of special importance in skin regeneration issues. N-acetylglucosamine, the product of CTS depolymerization, can initiate fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition in an ordered manner. CTS in organic organisms can be gradually depolymerized to N-acetylglucosamine, which starts the proliferation of fibroblasts and facilitates the deposition of ordered collagen. One of the CTS-based hemostatic commercial products is the HemCon bandage. The mechanism of action of HemCon is attaching to negatively charged cells of tissue as well as attracting negatively charged red blood cells and subsequently forming a tight cover over the wound (xxxx). Also, in this study, we decided to use CTS as the base material for the preparation of therapeutic gel for application in wound healing. Consequently, we introduced organic (carbon nanostructures) and inorganic (hydroxyapatite nanoparticles) nanofillers into the polymeric gel to produce polymeric systems with improved properties. In the context of tissue engineering, although biomaterial chemistry greatly affects the cell fate at the earliest phases, the internal architecture of a pharmaceutical dosage form also plays a main role, especially in the final phases, since it influences the tissue structure and its deposition kinetics. It has been reported previously that the regular and ordered geometry of the HAP scaffold can guide the bone tissue deposition within the pores with a lamellar structure, and the collagen fibers showed a parallel alignment (Scaglione, 2012). Accordingly, we hypothesized that the HAP with porous architecture, as well as elongated and rod-like structure, also can regulate the skin tissue to depose in an ordered structure with a parallel alignment of collagen fibers. Moreover, the high porosity structure (according to BET analysis) makes the mHAP NPs very potent materials in hemostasis and exudates absorption, which keeps a moist environment for stimulation of scar-free wound repair (Gonzalez de Oliveira, 2016). The issue of antibiotic resistance has motivated the development of new antibiotics for combatting pathogenic microorganisms. The mimesis of peroxidase activity prompted us to introduce carbon dot and its doped derivatives into the gels for endowing antibacterial properties.

Wounds are better when treated by CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gels compared to Ctrl and blank groups. It is possibly ascribed to the maintenance of a wet condition because of the presence of mHAP NPs, as we proved previously. (Derakhshi, 2023). The wet condition could effectively stimulate the healing process. Furthermore, it is suggested that calcium ions that we proved above to be released from mHAP NPs have a synergistic effect with phosphorus ions (released from mHAP NPs, the release of which was not tested in this study) can promote tissue regeneration. Moreover, the better health of wounds treated with CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gels may stem from the antibacterial activity of Cu.CDs, which diminishes the duration of the inflammation phase. As well as the antibacterial ability of copper, it had been previously pointed out that copper has the ability to modulate many growth factors and cytokine mechanisms of action and is practically involved in all phases of the wound healing process. More importantly, copper essentially helps in skin regeneration and angiogenesis, so it can accelerate the regeneration process by triggering vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and enhancing hypoxia-induced factor-1-alpha (HIF-1α) expression (Salvo and Sandoval, 2022). For this reason, we observed the topical application of the CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gel healed the wound significantly faster than the other group as on the 15th day post-operation, these wounds had almost healed entirely.

The significant promotion of wound contraction for CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gel is attributed to the presence of Cu.CDs, as well as mHAP NPs. mHAP NPs have the ability to increase fibroblast viability and proliferation. The greater antibacterial property of Cu.CD, which shortens the inflammatory phase plays a major supporting role in the wound-healing process. Overall, support of CTS gel by mHAP and Cu.CD NPs synergically imparted CTS gel, the ability of moisture maintenance and antibacterial performance that could significantly accelerate skin regeneration.

On day 5, the results of the pathology study manifestly suggested that CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group had the ability to hamper the inflammation as well as facilitate fibroblast proliferation and migration, and thus accelerate skin regeneration among the other tested groups. Re-epithelialization around the wounded tissue of CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD groups could be related to the synergistic activity of CTS, mHAP, and Cu.CD NPs to provoke tissue regeneration. mHAP NPs, which play a vital role in exudate absorption, can manage cell behaviors. Under appropriate conditions, copper deeply influences the regeneration process by overexpression of extracellular matrix molecules, including integrin, collagen, and fibrinogen, the main mediators that help in cell attachment to the extracellular matrix (Salvo and Sandoval, 2022). Therefore, the CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group showed a more integrated epidermis, suggesting that CTS/HAP + Cu.CD gel provides the best regeneration efficacy. The wound site of the Ctrl and blank groups is also full of monocytes, On day 10, while the volume of the inflammation decreased in three other groups. One reason could be the synergistic anti-inflammatory activity of the CTS, inorganic component mHAP, and both CDs. Soriente et al. reported that by increasing the concentration of an inorganic component, HAP, in their synthesized scaffolds, the production of pro-inflammatory (TGF-β) and anti-inflammatory (IL-4, IL-10) cytokines is modulated (Soriente, 2022). The inflammation has been diminished thoroughly in the CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group, which is due to the presence of copper in the gel formulation. The ability of mHAP NPs for cell proliferation and differentiation, which are the main topics in tissue engineering, promotes thin layers of neo-epithelium, keratin, and epidermis organization in CTS/HAP, CTS/HAP + CD, and CTS/HAP + Cu.CD groups. The difference in the skin reconstruction between CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group and two other main groups, CTS/HAP + CD and CTS/HAP, is attributed to the effective role of elements such as copper that have a critical role in the tissue regeneration process. On day 15, better tissue remodeling observed in the CTS/HAP + Cu.CD group is resulted from rapid passage of the inflammation stage and motivation of fibroblasts, keratinocytes, and endothelial cells’ migration due to the great collaboration between Cu.CDs, mHAP NPs, and released calcium ions were the main reasons for enhanced wound healing of the injured skin.

As traditional antibiotics indicate bacterial resistance, we decided to use CDs with properties that include enhanced membrane permeability, lack of efflux, and induction of various antibacterial mechanisms. Here, we doped them with N, F, and Cu to improve their antimicrobial properties. Moreover, in tissue regeneration, it has already been established that a geometrically well-organized scaffold can act as a template that effectively guides cells to the right location for highly ordered tissue deposition to occur. Accordingly, here we used mHAP NPs with well-organized geometry, thermal stability, chemical activity, and high biocompatibility because collagen deposition occurs in a very ordered manner. Therefore, we designed a CTS gel containing both Cu.CD and mHAP NPs aimed to evaluate the wound healing efficiency of these NPs simultaneously. We analyzed the antibacterial ability of the prepared CTS gel containing Cu.CD and mHAP NPs. Then, by evaluating the changes in the surface area of the wound during 15 days, as well as counting inflammatory cells and engorged blood vessels, evaluating the process of granulation tissue changes and re-epithelialization through pathological analysis, we evaluated their ability to heal wounds in a rat model.

It should be noted that for the in vivo study, the limitation is mainly related to the observation period that was limited (max. 15 days). As well, wound dressing detachment (for up to 10 % of animals) due to animal skipping is the other limitation. Moreover, it is expected that the presence of both HAP and Cu.CD induces the production of pro-inflammatory (TGF-β) and anti-inflammatory (IL-4, IL-10) cytokines. Therefore, another limitation of in vivo study is the absence of evaluation of the anti-inflammation markers' expression. These days, many researchers have emphasized the employment of ROS-scavenging components in the body during the tissue regeneration process. The antioxidants can protect wounds from bacterial infection and improve cell proliferation (Abedi et al., 2023 Feb 27). In the current study, we didn't use any antioxidants and we plan to evaluate the effect of an antioxidant in the proposed therapeutic system in a future study.

In this study, we prepared a therapeutic gel based on the CTS, which contains mHAP NPs and Cu.CDs. mHAP NPs and their released calcium ions can considerably improve cell proliferation and differentiation. In addition, they endow the gel with sufficient exudate absorption. The evidence obtained in the calcium release study sufficiently supports the hypothesis that the simultaneous use of two surfactants accelerates the release rate of calcium. Therefore, in addition to the advantages of the mHAP NPs, we also benefit from the effects of released calcium ions in the context of tissue engineering. Cu.CDs grant the gel exceptional antibacterial properties without the need for additional light and/or H2O2. They shorten the duration of the inflammatory phase in the wound healing process. Moreover, The presence of both mHAP NPs and Cu.CDs improved the rheological properties of the prepared gel, which is an assistance factor for better drug delivery and spreading over the skin surface. Thanks to the presence of both NPs, the prepared gel showed a significant advantage in wound healing. We hope that more research will be done to develop new devices using nanomaterials to advance tissue engineering and −regeneration. These days, many researchers have emphasized the employment of ROS-scavenging components in the body during the tissue regeneration process. CTS and natural polymers that are easy and inexpensive to extract and manipulate should receive more attention and research. Medical scientists should use more nature-inspired materials such as hydroxyapatite, collagen, gelatin, etc. Generally, the research should go towards materials that can leave the research phase and have the capability of skill-up and industrialization. On the other hand, considering the bacterial resistance of conventional antibiotics, it is necessary to conduct more research on new antibacterials that are based on nanoparticles and to study them in terms of toxicity and low-cost synthesis. Applied research should be considered, research that does not just lead to the publication of articles and can go to the market, and the science produced from them can be used by the general public.

Tooba Hallaj: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hamid Soraya: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Ata Abbasi: Investigation. Mina Tasoujlu: Investigation. Samaneh Pirmoradi: Investigation. Legha Ansari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
